We cannot forget how often, while Jesus was with them, there were arguments among the disciples about who would be the greatest; but now all those arguments had ended. They had prayed together more recently. If we want the Spirit poured out on us from above, let us all be of one mind. And despite differences in opinions and interests, as there were among those disciples, let us agree to love one another; for where people live together in unity, the Lord blesses them. A powerful wind came with great force. This was to show the strong influences and work of God’s Spirit on people's minds, and therefore on the world. In this way, the convictions of the Spirit prepare the way for his comforts; and the forceful blasts of that blessed wind prepare the soul for its gentle breezes. There appeared something like flaming fire, lighting on each of them, according to John Baptist's saying about Christ: He will baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire. The Spirit, like fire, melts the heart, burns away impurities, and kindles devout affections in the soul; within which, as in the fire on the altar, spiritual sacrifices are offered. They were all filled with the Holy Spirit, more than before. They were filled with the qualities of the Spirit, and more than ever under his influence; they were more detached from this world, and better acquainted with the next. They were more filled with the comforts of the Spirit, rejoiced more than ever in the love of Christ and the hope of heaven: in it all their sorrows and fears were overcome.

The difference in languages that arose at Babel has greatly hindered the spread of knowledge and religion. The people whom God first employed to spread the Christian religion could not have made progress without this gift, which proved that their authority was from God.

Peter’s sermon shows that he had fully recovered from his fall, and was fully restored to God's favor; for he who had denied Christ now boldly confessed him. His account of the miraculous pouring forth of the Spirit was designed to awaken the listeners to embrace faith in Christ, and to join themselves to his church. It was fulfilling Scripture, and the result of Christ’s resurrection and ascension, and proof of both. Though Peter was filled with the Holy Ghost, and spoke in other languages as the Spirit gave him utterance, he did not think to set aside the Scriptures. Christ's followers never learn above their Bible; and the Spirit is given, not to do away with the Scriptures, but to enable us to understand, approve, and obey them. Surely none will escape condemnation on the great day, except those who call upon the name of the Lord, in and through his Son Jesus Christ, as the Savior of sinners, and the Judge of all people.

From this gift of the Holy Ghost, Peter preaches about Jesus: and here is the history of Christ. Here is an account of his death and sufferings, which they witnessed only a few weeks before. His death is considered God's act; and it is an act of wonderful grace and wisdom. Thus Divine justice must be satisfied, God and people brought together again, and Christ himself glorified, according to an eternal plan that could not be changed. And as the people’s act, it was an act of terrible sin and foolishness. Christ's resurrection removed the shame of his death; Peter speaks extensively about this. Christ was God’s Holy One, set apart for his service in the work of redemption. His death and sufferings should be, not to him only, but to all who are his, the entrance to a blessed life forevermore. This event took place as foretold, and the apostles were witnesses. Nor did the resurrection rest on this alone; Christ had poured upon his disciples miraculous gifts and Divine influences, of which they witnessed the effects. Through the Savior, the ways of life are made known; and we are encouraged to expect God’s presence, and his favor forevermore. All this comes from a firm belief that Jesus is the Lord, and the anointed Savior.

From the first delivery of that Divine message, it appeared that there was Divine power going with it; and thousands were brought to obey faith. But neither Peter's words, nor the miracle they witnessed, could have produced such effects, had not the Holy Spirit been given. Sinners, when their eyes are opened, cannot but be pricked in their hearts for sin, cannot but feel an inward uneasiness. The apostle urged them to repent of their sins, and openly to declare their belief in Jesus as the Messiah, by being baptized in his name. Thus professing their faith in Him, they would receive forgiveness of their sins, and share in the gifts and qualities of the Holy Spirit. To separate from wicked people is the only way to save ourselves from them. Those who repent of their sins, and give themselves to Jesus Christ, must prove their sincerity by breaking off from the wicked. We must save ourselves from them; which means avoiding them with fear and reverence. By God’s grace three thousand people accepted the gospel invitation. There is no doubt that the gift of the Holy Ghost, which they all received, and from which no true believer has ever been excluded, was that Spirit of adoption, that converting, guiding, sanctifying grace, which is bestowed upon all members of our heavenly Father's family. Repentance and forgiveness of sins are still preached to the chief of sinners, in the Redeemer’s name; the Holy Spirit still seals the blessing on the believer’s heart; still the encouraging promises are for us and our children; and still the blessings are offered to all who are far away.

In these verses we have the history of the truly original church, of its first days; its state was that of infancy indeed, but like that, the state of its greatest innocence. They kept close to holy practices, and abounded in piety and devotion; for Christianity, when admitted with power, will dispose the soul to communion with God in all those ways wherein he has appointed us to meet him, and has promised to meet us. The greatness of the event raised them above the world, and the Holy Ghost filled them with such love, as made each one to be to another as to himself, and so made all things common, not by destroying property, but doing away selfishness, and causing charity. And God who moved them to it, knew that they were quickly to be driven from their possessions in Judea. The Lord, day by day, inclined the hearts of more to embrace the gospel; not merely professors, but such as were actually brought into a state of acceptance with God, being made partakers of renewing grace. Those whom God has designed for eternal salvation, will surely be brought to Christ, until the earth is filled with the knowledge of his glory.
